title: Energy Systems and Sustainability
whatIsIt: A way of understanding how energy is produced, transformed, distributed, used and managed in relation to environmental and social wellbeing.
whyItMatters: Students need to see that energy is not only a science topic. It shapes climate, health, technology, transport, equity and the future of communities.
example: A city that shifts from coal power to solar, storage and efficient buildings changes both its energy system and its environmental impact.
thinkAboutIt: Why is a sustainable energy system about more than simply using renewable energy?

title: Forms of Energy
whatIsIt: The main ways energy appears in systems, such as light, heat, motion, chemical energy and electrical energy.
whyItMatters: Students understand energy better when they can recognise that it changes form while still remaining part of the same system.
example: A torch changes chemical energy in a battery into electrical energy and then into light and heat.
thinkAboutIt: Why is it useful to identify the form of energy at each stage of a system?

title: Energy Transfer
whatIsIt: The movement of energy from one object or system to another.
whyItMatters: Energy systems only work when energy moves. Understanding transfer helps students explain heating, motion, electricity and system losses.
example: When a kettle boils water, electrical energy is transferred into thermal energy.
thinkAboutIt: Where does energy go when a system seems inefficient?

title: Energy Conversion
whatIsIt: The process of changing energy from one form into another.
whyItMatters: Most real systems involve several conversions, and each stage may include useful output and losses.
example: A wind turbine converts kinetic energy in moving air into electrical energy.
thinkAboutIt: Why do energy conversions often produce unwanted heat?

title: Energy Efficiency
whatIsIt: A measure of how much input energy becomes useful output.
whyItMatters: Efficiency helps students see that sustainability is not only about new energy sources, but also about using less energy for the same purpose.
example: LED lights provide the same brightness as older bulbs while using less electricity.
thinkAboutIt: Why can improving efficiency sometimes be easier than building new power stations?

title: Entropy
whatIsIt: A concept in thermodynamics linked to disorder and the tendency for energy to become less available for useful work.
whyItMatters: Entropy explains why perfectly efficient systems do not exist and why energy quality matters.
example: A hot drink cools down naturally as energy spreads into the surrounding air.
thinkAboutIt: Why is some energy still present in a system but no longer easy to use?

title: Solar Radiation
whatIsIt: Energy emitted by the sun in the form of electromagnetic waves.
whyItMatters: Solar radiation drives weather, climate, photosynthesis and most renewable energy systems on Earth.
example: Plants capture solar energy during photosynthesis and solar panels convert part of it into electricity.
thinkAboutIt: Why can the sun power both natural ecosystems and modern technology?

title: Photosynthesis
whatIsIt: The process by which plants use sunlight, carbon dioxide and water to produce glucose and oxygen.
whyItMatters: It shows how energy enters ecosystems and links energy systems to the carbon cycle and life on Earth.
example: Green plants in a forest store solar energy in chemical form.
thinkAboutIt: Why is photosynthesis one of the most important energy processes on Earth?

title: Ecosystem Energy Flow
whatIsIt: The movement of energy through living systems from producers to consumers and decomposers.
whyItMatters: Students see that ecosystems depend on constant energy flow and that energy is lost between trophic levels.
example: Energy moves from grass to rabbits to foxes, with less useful energy available at each step.
thinkAboutIt: Why are food chains usually short?

title: Electricity Generation
whatIsIt: The process of producing electrical energy from primary energy sources such as coal, gas, sunlight, wind or water.
whyItMatters: Electricity powers modern life, so students need to understand where it comes from and how it is produced.
example: A power station may use steam to turn turbines connected to a generator.
thinkAboutIt: Why is electricity so useful compared with many other forms of energy?

title: Power Plants
whatIsIt: Facilities that convert primary energy into electrical power at scale.
whyItMatters: They help students connect science concepts to real infrastructure and understand trade-offs between different generation methods.
example: Coal plants, gas plants, hydro stations and solar farms are all power generation systems.
thinkAboutIt: Why do different power plants have different environmental impacts?

title: Electricity Grids
whatIsIt: Networks that transport electricity from producers to users through transmission and distribution systems.
whyItMatters: Students often know where electricity is used but not how it reaches them. The grid makes the energy system visible.
example: Electricity generated at a power plant travels through high-voltage lines before reaching homes and schools.
thinkAboutIt: Why is the grid essential even when a place has its own solar panels?

title: Energy Consumption
whatIsIt: The use of energy by households, transport, industry, agriculture and digital systems.
whyItMatters: Demand shapes the whole energy system. Students need to see that sustainability involves changing how energy is used as well as how it is produced.
example: Air conditioning, internet services, factories and transport all contribute to total energy demand.
thinkAboutIt: Why does reducing demand matter as much as changing supply?

title: Fossil Fuel Energy
whatIsIt: Energy obtained from coal, oil and natural gas formed from ancient organic matter.
whyItMatters: Fossil fuels have powered industrial development, but their combustion releases greenhouse gases and pollutants.
example: Petrol cars and many thermal power plants rely on fossil fuels.
thinkAboutIt: Why do societies still depend on fossil fuels even though cleaner alternatives exist?

title: Carbon Cycle
whatIsIt: The movement of carbon through the atmosphere, living things, oceans and soils.
whyItMatters: The carbon cycle links energy systems, ecosystems and climate change.
example: Plants absorb carbon dioxide during photosynthesis, while combustion releases it back into the atmosphere.
thinkAboutIt: How does burning fossil fuels disrupt the natural balance of the carbon cycle?

title: Carbon Emissions
whatIsIt: The release of carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases into the atmosphere, especially from energy use.
whyItMatters: Carbon emissions are one of the clearest links between energy systems and global environmental change.
example: Driving a petrol car releases carbon dioxide because fuel combustion produces greenhouse gases.
thinkAboutIt: Why are emissions often treated as a system problem rather than only an individual problem?

title: Greenhouse Effect
whatIsIt: A natural process in which certain gases trap heat in Earth's atmosphere.
whyItMatters: Students need to understand the greenhouse effect before they can explain climate change.
example: Without the greenhouse effect, Earth would be much too cold for most current life forms.
thinkAboutIt: Why does a natural process become a problem when greenhouse gas levels rise too much?

title: Climate Change
whatIsIt: Long-term changes in Earth's climate system driven strongly today by human greenhouse gas emissions.
whyItMatters: It shows students why energy systems matter beyond science classrooms, because climate affects health, biodiversity, food and livelihoods.
example: More frequent extreme heat and intense weather events are linked to climate change.
thinkAboutIt: Why does solving climate change require both technological and social change?

title: Renewable Energy
whatIsIt: Energy from sources that are naturally replenished, such as sunlight, wind, moving water, biomass and geothermal heat.
whyItMatters: Renewables can reduce dependence on fossil fuels, but they also require infrastructure, storage and planning.
example: Solar farms and wind turbines generate electricity without burning fuel.
thinkAboutIt: Why does renewable energy still need strong systems and policies to work well?

title: Solar Energy Systems
whatIsIt: Technologies that capture sunlight to produce electricity or heat.
whyItMatters: Solar energy is one of the most visible renewable systems and helps students connect sunlight to practical energy solutions.
example: Rooftop solar panels can help a school produce some of its own electricity.
thinkAboutIt: Why does solar energy often need storage or grid support?

title: Wind Energy Systems
whatIsIt: Technologies that convert the kinetic energy of moving air into electricity.
whyItMatters: Wind systems show how natural motion can become useful electrical power at scale.
example: Wind farms use turbines to drive generators when air moves across the blades.
thinkAboutIt: Why is wind energy strong in some places and less suitable in others?

title: Hydropower Systems
whatIsIt: Energy systems that convert the energy of moving or falling water into electricity.
whyItMatters: Hydropower helps students see that renewable systems can be reliable and large-scale, but also have environmental trade-offs.
example: A hydroelectric dam uses flowing water to turn turbines connected to generators.
thinkAboutIt: Why can hydropower be both a renewable solution and an environmental challenge?

title: Bioenergy Systems
whatIsIt: Energy systems that use biological material such as crops, waste or plant matter as fuel.
whyItMatters: Bioenergy links energy, agriculture, land use and the carbon cycle.
example: Biogas can be produced from food waste or organic waste streams.
thinkAboutIt: When is bioenergy sustainable, and when might it create new problems?

title: Energy Storage
whatIsIt: The capture of energy for use at a later time.
whyItMatters: Storage helps balance supply and demand, especially when renewable sources are variable.
example: A battery stores solar electricity produced during the day for later use at night.
thinkAboutIt: Why is storage so important in renewable energy systems?

title: Batteries
whatIsIt: Devices that store chemical energy and convert it into electrical energy when needed.
whyItMatters: Batteries are central to portable electronics, electric vehicles and many renewable energy systems.
example: A home battery can store electricity from rooftop solar panels.
thinkAboutIt: Why are batteries useful, but not the only answer to energy storage?

title: Smart Grids
whatIsIt: Electricity networks that use digital technologies to monitor, manage and optimise energy flow.
whyItMatters: Smart grids show students that future energy systems rely on information as well as electricity.
example: A smart grid can respond to demand peaks and coordinate distributed solar and battery storage.
thinkAboutIt: Why do complex energy systems need data and intelligent control?

title: Electric Vehicles
whatIsIt: Vehicles powered partly or fully by electricity stored in batteries.
whyItMatters: They help students connect transport energy use to wider energy transitions, infrastructure and emissions reduction.
example: An electric bus may reduce urban air pollution if charged from a cleaner electricity supply.
thinkAboutIt: Why do electric vehicles only become truly low-carbon when the wider energy system also changes?

title: Energy Transition
whatIsIt: The shift from high-carbon energy systems to cleaner, more sustainable systems.
whyItMatters: This concept brings together science, policy, infrastructure, economics and behaviour change.
example: A country may retire coal plants while investing in solar, wind, storage and grid upgrades.
thinkAboutIt: Why is the energy transition both a technical and a social challenge?

title: Sustainable Energy Systems
whatIsIt: Energy systems designed to meet present needs without undermining environmental stability, social wellbeing or future generations.
whyItMatters: This is the big picture goal that connects all the other concepts students learn.
example: A sustainable energy system may combine renewable power, efficient buildings, public transport and circular material use.
thinkAboutIt: What would a truly sustainable energy system need besides clean electricity?

title: Buildings Energy Use
whatIsIt: Energy consumed in buildings for heating, cooling, lighting, appliances and other services.
whyItMatters: Students can relate this concept directly to homes and schools, making the topic immediate and practical.
example: Better insulation and efficient air conditioning can reduce a building's energy demand.
thinkAboutIt: Why can energy efficiency in buildings have such a large effect on sustainability?

title: Transport Energy
whatIsIt: Energy used to move people and goods by road, rail, air and sea.
whyItMatters: Transport is one of the clearest ways students see energy use in daily life, and it is a major source of emissions in many places.
example: Cars, buses, trains and planes each rely on different fuels and systems.
thinkAboutIt: Why is changing transport energy systems often harder than changing one individual vehicle?

title: Industrial Energy
whatIsIt: Energy used in manufacturing, processing, mining and other industrial activities.
whyItMatters: Industry often requires large amounts of energy and high-temperature heat, making decarbonisation more challenging.
example: Steel and cement production use energy-intensive processes.
thinkAboutIt: Why are some parts of industry harder to decarbonise than homes or offices?

title: Digital Energy
whatIsIt: Energy used by digital devices, networks, data centres and online systems.
whyItMatters: Students often underestimate how much energy supports digital life, from streaming to cloud computing.
example: Data centres require electricity for both computing and cooling.
thinkAboutIt: Why does invisible digital infrastructure still have a physical energy footprint?
